forecasts.reasoning: Although the 'Huaweigate' scandal has led to requests to lift the immunity of four active MEPs (Attard, Minchev, Martusciello, De Meo), they are highly unlikely to resign in the short term. As seen in the 2022 Qatargate scandal (e.g., Eva Kaili, Marc Tarabella), implicated MEPs strongly prefer to retain their seats; resigning would immediately strip them of their parliamentary immunity, expediting their potential arrest and prosecution, and cost them their salaries. Furthermore, the targeted MEPs have publicly denied wrongdoing and indicated their intent to defend themselves. The legal process in the Parliament to waive immunity typically takes months, meaning there is no immediate procedural mechanism forcing them out in June 2025. However, because the European Parliament has 720 members, there is always a baseline probability that at least one MEP might resign during any 21-day window for entirely unrelated reasons, such as accepting a national political appointment, entering local government, or dealing with personal/health issues. Accounting for both the unlikelihood of a scandal-driven resignation and the baseline rate of normal parliamentary turnover, the probability is set around 18%.

The phrase "reverse purchasing" translates a Chinese term (反向代购) recently promoted by state media to describe tourists or cross-border buyers purchasing mainland Chinese goods. Western outlets like the New York Times generally do not adopt niche, direct translations of Chinglish terms unless they are explicitly analyzing the state-media narrative in a dedicated feature. The alternative phrase "reverse purchase" primarily describes niche financial/real-estate transactions (e.g., HECM reverse purchase mortgages) or functions as a shorthand/misnomer for a "reverse repurchase agreement," neither of which frequently appear in standard NYT reporting. 

The primary deciding factor is the extremely narrow resolution window of just roughly three weeks (June 10 to June 30, 2025). The base rate of the NYT publishing these exact phrases within any given three-week period is exceptionally low, as historical site searches reveal virtually no regular usage of either precise string. Without indication of a scheduled long-form piece on this exact topic set for that exact 21-day period, the likelihood of a match is near the minimum allowable probability.

The main uncertainty is whether Venezuela will keep X blocked through June 2025, as the other six countries (China, Iran, Myanmar, North Korea, Russia, Turkmenistan) are run by heavily entrenched authoritarian regimes with long-standing bans on the platform. Venezuela’s block was initiated in August 2024 as a "10-day" ban following the contested election and a public feud between Nicolás Maduro and Elon Musk. Because Musk is unlikely to comply with Venezuelan government demands to remove content or appoint representatives (unlike in Brazil, where X complied due to a much larger market and different judicial circumstances), and relations between Maduro and Musk/the US are likely to remain deeply antagonistic, the block will probably become permanent by default. The remaining risk accounts for the possibility that Venezuela quietly lifts the block as domestic tensions cool, or that Wikipedia restructures the article in a way that creates ambiguity for the question's resolution criteria. Overall, there is a strong likelihood all seven countries will still be listed as currently blocking X.

President Yamandú Orsi of Uruguay was formally invited by Brazilian President Lula da Silva to attend the BRICS summit in Rio de Janeiro in July 2025. Following the invitation, Uruguay's Foreign Minister Mario Lubetkin publicly praised the opportunity, stating that participating "will mean a change in the quality of the dialogue between our country and the great emerging countries of the South." Given Orsi's center-left political alignment with Lula, the geographic proximity of Rio de Janeiro, Uruguay's pending finalization of its accession to the BRICS New Development Bank, and strong economic incentives to engage with BRICS nations (such as China), it is highly likely that Orsi will accept. While there is a small chance diplomatic pressure from the U.S. might cause him to send a lower-level delegation (like the Foreign Minister) instead of attending in person, the public and positive governmental reception of the invitation strongly points to a personal attendance. Therefore, an announcement confirming his attendance before July 1, 2025, is highly probable.

As of the knowledge cutoff date (June 5, 2025), Miami Mayor Francis Suarez has stated he is "considering" a run for Florida Governor but has not committed, acknowledging that former President Trump's endorsement of Representative Byron Donalds "makes it hard" for other candidates in the GOP primary. For this question to resolve as "Yes", Suarez would need to officially announce his candidacy within the next 26 days (before July 1, 2025). Given the steep uphill battle in a primary against a Trump-endorsed frontrunner, Suarez's low polling numbers (1-5%), and the very short remaining time window, it is highly unlikely he will formally launch a gubernatorial campaign before the July 1 deadline.

The knowledge cutoff is June 5, 2025. The resolution deadline is July 1, 2025, leaving only a 25-day window for an announcement. 

While there was intense speculation earlier in the year that Casey DeSantis might run to succeed her husband as Florida Governor in 2026, recent developments make an imminent announcement highly unlikely. First, President Trump issued an early endorsement for Rep. Byron Donalds, who has formally declared his candidacy and recently opened up a 19-point lead over DeSantis in a May 2025 poll. Second, DeSantis has recently faced negative press and legislative scrutiny regarding her "Hope Florida" nonprofit. 

Third, political insiders and operatives reported in mid-May that she is now "unlikely to challenge" Donalds and that local "chatter has certainly died way down." Lastly, when asked about her plans on May 14, 2025, she deflected by explicitly noting that the qualifying deadline is "more than a year away," which strongly implies she feels no pressure to make a decision anytime soon. Given the short time remaining until the July 1 cutoff, her loss of political momentum, and her own signals indicating a delayed timeline, the probability of her announcing a gubernatorial bid in the next few weeks is very low.
